elements.sent-2411-001: Many properties of Boolean functions can be tested far more efficiently than the function itself can be learned.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, this dramatic advantage often disappears when testers are limited to random samples of the function---a natural setting for data science---rather than queries.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work we investigate the quantum version of this ``data science scenario'': quantum algorithms that test properties of a function $f$ solely from quantum data in the form of copies of the function state $\ket{f}\propto \sum_x\ket{x,f(x)}$.
elements.sent-2411-004: For three well-established properties, we show that the speedup lost when restricting classical testers to sample access can be essentially recovered by quantum testers that use such quantum data.
elements.sent-2411-005: For monotonicity testing, we give a quantum algorithm that uses $\tilde{\mathcal{O (n^2)$ copies of the corresponding function state as compared to the $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n )}$ samples required for testing from classical data.}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: We also present $\mathcal{O (1)$-copy testers for symmetry and triangle-freeness, comparing favorably to the classical lower bounds of $\Omega(n^{1/4 )$ and $\Omega(n)$ samples respectively \cite{alon2016active .}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: These algorithms are time-efficient and necessarily include techniques beyond the Fourier sampling approaches applied to earlier testing problems.
elements.sent-2411-008: Taken together, these results make the case for a general study of the advantages afforded by quantum data for property testing.
elements.sent-2411-009: We contribute to this larger project by complementing our upper bounds with a nontrivial lower bound of $\Omega(1/\varepsilon)$ for monotonicity testing from quantum data in the proximity regime $\varepsilon\leq\mathcal{O (n^{-3/2 )$.}}

elements.sent-2411-012: In property testing we consider a subset $\mc P$ of the set of all Boolean functions $f:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$ and aim to find fast algorithms for deciding (with high probability) whether an unknown function $f$ has property $\mc P$ or is $\epsilon$-far from having property $\mc P$; that is, we wish to decide between \begin{equation}Case (i)\quad f\in \mc P\quad\qquad or\quad\qquad Case (ii)\quad\min_{g\in \mc P}\|f-g\|_1\geq \epsilon\,,\end{equation} promised one of these is the case.
elements.sent-2411-014: Property testing began in the context of program checking \cite{blum1990self, rubinfeld1996robust , where it was shown that only $\mc O(1)$ queries to $f$ are needed to determine (with high probability) whether $f$ is linear or is $\Theta(1)$-far from linear---which compares very favorably to the $\Omega(n)$ query lower bound for learning linear functions.}
elements.sent-2411-015: The extreme query efficiency of property testing algorithms soon after played a critical role in interactive proofs and PCP theorems \cite{arora1998probabilistic, arora1998proof, dinur2007pcp .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Since then property testing has developed into a rich landscape of access models, complexity regimes, and separations \cite{fischer2004art,Rubinfeld2007,DBLP:journals/fttcs/Ron09,DBLP:conf/propertytesting/Sudan10,Goldreich_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: One of the promises of this broad view of property testing, identified very early on \cite{goldreich1998property , is its potential in data analysis and machine learning: one could run inexpensive property testing algorithms to guide the choice of which long-running learning algorithm to then use.}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, the dramatic complexity advantage of testing over learning often disappears in the natural access model for data analysis and machine learning, where fresh queries to $f$ cannot be made and only a limited dataset $\{(x_j,f(x_j)\}_j$ of random samples from $f$ is available.
elements.sent-2411-019: This setting is known as passive or sample-based testing \cite{goldreich1998property .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Indeed, much work in passive testing has proved lower bounds growing with $n$, which when contrasted to query-based testing upper bounds can be viewed as expressions of the power of specific, property-dependent query access for fast algorithms; compare among the ``Classical'' columns in \Cref{tab:q-vs-c .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In fact, Blais and Yoshida \cite{blaisyoshida2019 showed that if a Boolean property can be tested from $\mc O(1)$ random samples, then the property is of a rather restricted kind.\footnote{In particular, such a property is only a function of the conditional expected values $\E_x[f(x)|x\in S_j]$ of $f$ for sets $S_j$ forming a constant-cardinality partition of the hypercube, $S_1\sqcup\cdots\sqcup S_{\mc O(1) =\{0,1\}^n$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-044: Monotonicity has been of central importance in the classical property testing literature \cite{Goldreich2000, Belovs2015, DBLP:journals/siamcomp/KhotMS18 , and we show it can be efficiently tested from quantum data.}

elements.paragraph-2411-004: Upper bound for passive quantum monotonicity testing.
elements.sent-2411-075: We take a Fourier sampling approach to monotonicity testing.
elements.sent-2411-076: Our starting point is the central insight from \cite{Goldreich2000 : If $f:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$ is exactly $\varepsilon$-far from monotone, then \begin{equation}\label{eq:monotonicity-soundness-intro} \frac{\varepsilon}{n} \leq \Pr_{x\sim\{0,1\}^n, i\sim [n]}\left[(x_i = 0 \land (f(x) > f(x+ e_i)))\lor (x_i = 1 \land (f(x)< f(x+ e_i)))\right] \leq 2\varepsilon\, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Thus, to test monotonicity, it is enough to estimate the probability in \Cref{eq:monotonicity-soundness-intro to accuracy $\sim \varepsilon/n$.}
elements.sent-2411-078: To do this, we first observe that the probability of interest can be rewritten in terms of Fourier-analytic quantities as $\frac{1 {2n}\mathbf{I [(-1)^f] - \frac{1 {2n}\sum_{i=1 ^n \widehat{(-1)^f (\{i\})$, where $\mathbf{I}$ denotes the total influence.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: The total influence $\mathbf{I [(-1)^f]$ equals the expected size of a subset $S$ sampled from the Fourier distribution $\{|\widehat{(-1)^f (S)|^2\}_{S\subseteq [n]}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: So, via quantum Fourier sampling \cite{bernstein1997complexity , we can, with high success probability, estimate $\mathbf{I [(-1)^f]$ to accuracy $\sim \varepsilon$ from $\mathcal{O (n^2/\varepsilon^2)$ function state copies.}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Also, every $\widehat{(-1)^f (\{i\})$ can be estimated to accuracy $\sim\varepsilon/n$ from $\mathcal{O (n^2/\varepsilon^2)$ classical samples with high success probability.}}

elements.section-2411-004: Separating passive quantum from query-based classical property testing
elements.sent-2411-510: In this section we give a property for which classical queries have exponential advantage over quantum testing from function states.
elements.sent-2411-511: This property is closely related to the inability of quantum computers to measure the intersection of three subset states, as we explain at the end of the section.
elements.sent-2411-512: There exist two sets of Boolean functions $F_0,F_1$ such that \begin{equation}\min_{f_0\in F_0,f_1\in F_1} \|f_0-f_1\|_1\geq \frac{1}{64}\end{equation} and such that:.
elements.sent-2411-513: Any passive quantum tester requires $\Omega(2^{n/2 )$ copies of a function state to distinguish $F_0$ and $F_1$ with constant probability $2/3$.}
elements.sent-2411-514: The families $F_0$ and $F_1$ arise from certain encodings of triples of subsets $A,B,C\subseteq\{0,1\}^n$.
elements.sent-2411-515: Consider the class of Boolean functions $f_{(A,B,C) :\{0,1\}^{n+2}\to\{0,1\}$ on $n+2$ bits parameterized by subsets $A,B,C\subseteq\{0,1\}^n$ and defined as follows: \begin{equation}f_{(A,B,C)}(x,a)= 1_{x\in A} a = 00 1_{x\in B} a = 01 1_{x\in C} a = 10 0 a = 11. \end{equation} With $A,B,C$ drawn uniformly from subsets of $\{0,1\}^n$, we define two function state ensembles $\{\ket{f_{(A,B,C) }\}_{A,B,C}$ and $\{\ket{f_{(A,B,A\Delta B) }\}_{A,B}$, with their average $t$-copy states given by \begin{equation}\mc E_0 = \E_{A,B,C} \left[\Ket{f_{(A,B,C)}}\Bra{f_{(A,B,C)}}^{\otimes t}\right],\qquad \mc E_1 = \E_{A,B} \left[\Ket{f_{(A,B,A\Delta B)}}\Bra{f_{(A,B,A\Delta B)}}^{\otimes t}\right]\, .\end{equation}.}}}
elements.formula-2411-091: \begin{equation}f_{(A,B,C)}(x,a)= 1_{x\in A} a = 00 1_{x\in B} a = 01 1_{x\in C} a = 10 0 a = 11. \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-092: \begin{equation}\mc E_0 = \E_{A,B,C} \left[\Ket{f_{(A,B,C)}}\Bra{f_{(A,B,C)}}^{\otimes t}\right],\qquad \mc E_1 = \E_{A,B} \left[\Ket{f_{(A,B,A\Delta B)}}\Bra{f_{(A,B,A\Delta B)}}^{\otimes t}\right]\, .\end{equation}

For monotonicity, symmetry, and triangle-freeness, passive quantum testing from function states is (at least) exponentially easier than passive classical testing from samples and at most polynomially harder than classical testing from queries. The testing problem derived from 3-fold intersection estimation is complementary to the Forrelation problem in that quantum function/phase states and classical queries swap roles in the exponential separation. }\label{table:results-overview} \end{center}

elements.sent-2411-1013: Classically, passive triangle-freeness testing requires at least $\Omega(2^{n/3 )$ samples.}
elements.sent-2411-1014: We give a brief proof sketch for this lower bound.
elements.sent-2411-1015: Suppose we obtain $q$ samples with inputs drawn i.i.d.~uniformly at random from $\{0, 1\}^n$.
elements.sent-2411-1016: To witness a triangle, we must observe a linearly dependent triple $(x, y, x+y)$.
elements.sent-2411-1017: For any distinct pair $(x, y)$, the probability that $x+y$ also appears among the remaining $q-2$ samples is at most $\frac{q-2 {2^n}$.}
elements.sent-2411-1018: A union bound over all possible pairs $(x, y)$ gives us an upper bound of $\frac{q^3 {2^n}$ on the probability that any triple is linearly dependent.}
elements.sent-2411-1019: Therefore, we need at least $\Omega(2^{\frac{n {3 )$ samples for a constant probability of witnessing a triangle (which is required for triangle-freeness testing).}}}
